operations.text: The city should require large apartment buildings — [G1: define which buildings count as "large"] — to install smart cooling systems for heat waves, but only if tenant privacy is protected and landlords receive [G4: define subsidy type, amount, and funding source].
Extreme heat is becoming more dangerous — [G2: add local evidence or trend data] — and many older buildings trap heat overnight.
The system should not record conversations, track tenant movement, or share apartment-level data with landlords except when there is an [G3: define when apartment-level data may be shared].

If buildings with the system show higher eviction or rent-increase rates, the city should pause enforcement and investigate after [G5: define audit trigger, comparison threshold, and investigation process].
Therefore, smart cooling requirements are a fair, practical, and necessary response to extreme heat, with unresolved criteria: [G6: define criteria for fair, practical, and necessary].
